Farn Por-rrrcel PRACTTcES C ouurssroN
P.O. Box 807 o 428 J Street . Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
August 12,2002
Marguerite P. Battersby, City Attorney
City of Adelanto
o/o Brunick, Battersby, McElhaneY
& Beckett
Post Office Box 6425
San Bemardino, CA 92412
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-02-141
Dear Ms. Battersby:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Since you are requesting
a{vice about another person's duties under the Act, based upon your duty as the city
attorney for the City of Adelanto ("City") to provide City officials with advice, rather
than upon their specific authoization, we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance. (Regulation t 8329(c)(1 ), copy attached.)
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct
that may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts you
present; the Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission") does not act as a finder
of fact when providing assistance. (In re Oglesby (197 5) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ; Gov. Cocle
83 r 14.)
, 
QUESTION
Do Mayor Tristan Pelayes and Councilmembers William Hartzand Gerald
Steffanus have a conflict of interest disqualifying them from participating in closed
session discussions and voting upon matters, including whether to retain separate counsel
for the mayor, regarding litigation filed by CouncilmembersHartz and Steffanus against
the City, and against fvfi. pefayes in his capacity as the City's mayot?2
I Govemment Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
' You also ask about the scope of the attomey-client privilege and the mechanics for the City's
waiver of the attorney client privilege . Since the Commission's jurisdiction is limited to matters arising
under the Act, we can offer no advice on your questions regarding this privilege.


File No. I-A2-l4l
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
Mayor Pelayes may participate in closed session discussions and vote upon
matters regarding this litigation, including the decision whether to retain separate counsel
to assume his defense. Govemment Code section 995 requires the City to defend Mayor
Pelayes in the pending litigation, upon his request, unless certain statutory exceptions
apply. Consequently, his economic interest in his personal finances is not involved in
litigation decisions brought before the city council. Moreover, the decision whether to
retain separate counsel is one that falls under the exception of regulation 18702.4 andis
considered a decision affecting his own compensation, or terms and conditions of his own
employment or contract. Under this exception, Mayor Pelayes is free to vote on the city
council's decision whether to obtain separate counsel for his defense in this litigation.
Councilmembers Hartz and Steffanus, as plaintiffs in the litigation, have a conflict
of interest ordinarily barring their involvement in governmental decisions regarding this
litigation if it is reasonably foreseeable flrat the decisions will result in a material
financial effect on their personal finances. Ho\rrever, under the facts provided the City
will lack a quorum if both of these council members are ba:red from involvement. Under
the "legally required participation" exception, one of these two council members may be
randomly selected to be involved in the governmental decisions regarding this litigation.3
FACTS
The City of Adelanto is a charter City governed by a five-member city council,
elected at large. The City's mayor is a member of and appointed by the city council. In
January of 2002, a member of the city council resigned and the resultant vacancy was not
filled by appointrnent within the 30-day period allowed by law. Consequently, this
Yacancy will remain open until filled under the next regular municipal election, to be held
in November of 2002.
The City has been investigating whether to disband the Adelanto Police
Department and contract with the San Bernardino County Sheriff s Department
("Sheriff s Department") to provide local police services for the City. At a November
2001 city council meeting, the city manager was directed to enter into negotiations with
the Sheriff s Department concerning this assumption of local police duties. Ultimately,
these negotiations resulted in a contract between the City and the SherifFs Department,
and the contract was ratified by a city council vote. Mayor Pelayes participated in these
negotiations and in the city council vote ratifying this contract. You state in a telephone
conversation with our staff that prior to his participation, Mayor Pelayes consulted with
your predecessor in office and obtained advice that he did not have a conflict of interest
barring his participation in these negotiations and in the subsequent vote ratiffing the
contract.
3 We address only whether one of the two council member's involvement will be permitted under
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. We do not address rvhether any other provisions of law
would bar their involvement.


File No. I-02-l4l
Page No. 3
On May 9,2002, Councilmembers Hartz and Steffanus, in their capacity as
private citrzens,joined local taxpayer Mark Cannon to file a petition for writ of mandate
in the San Bernardino Superior Court against Mayor Pelayes and the City to set aside this
contract. The suit claims that Mayor Pelayes has a conflict of interest in that he is a
reserve deputy sheriff for San Bemardino County and also a county counsel. The suit
alleges that this ostensible conflict of interest tainted the negotiations and subsequent
ratification of this contract, and seeks a writ to set aside the contract. In addition, the suit
seeks recovery of legal costs and "such other relief as may be just and proper."
The city council is contemplating a closed session to discuss the pending litigation
and to adopt strategy. You state that it is possible that Mayor Pelayes will seek separate
counsel appointed at City expense due to the possible separate litigation interests of the
City and Mayor Pelayes.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard
analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualiffing conflict of interest
(Regulation 18700, subdivisions (b)(1) - (8), which is discussed below. The general
rule, however, is that a conflict of interest may occur whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision which may reasonably be foreseen as materially affecting one or
more of his or her financial interests.
l. & z.Are Councilmembers Hartz and Steffanus and Mayor Pelayes public
ollicials who will make, participate in making, or influence a governmental
decision?
The conflict-of-interest prohibition applies only to public officials. As elected
me.mbers of the city council, Mr. Hartz, Mr. Steffanus and Mayor Pelayes are public
officials. (Section 82048; regulation 18701(a).) Further, as council members, unless
disqualified under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act, they will make,
participate in making, and influence govemmental decisions. (Section 87100; regulations
r8702.r-r8702.3.)
Making a litigation decision (e.g., amendment of pleadings, commencement of
additional litigation, responding to pre-trial motions, engaging in trial strategy and tactics,
making and responding to settlement offers, appealing district courl decisions, etc.) is
considered to be making a govemmental decision for purposes of the Act. (Randolph
Advice Letter, No. A-01-109.) However, making a purely procedural decision, such as
sch.eduling a court date, is not making or participating in making a governmental
decision. Advice, recorrrmendations or coflrmunications to fellow members of the city
council made with the intent to influence the outcome of a vote on amatter conceming
litigation against the City or its public officials would comprise participating in making,
or influencing, a governmental decision. (Luna Advice Letter, No. 4-02-018.) Thus, by
participating in a lawsuit as plaintiffs or defendants in their official capacity, public


File No. I-02-l4l
Page No. 4
officials are typically engaging in conduct regulated by the conflict -of-interest provisions
of the Act. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3); O'Brien Advice Letter, No. A-99-111a.)
Some specific exceptions exist under regulation 18702.4 whereby the
Commission has determined that public officials are allowed to make, participate in
making and influence decisions affecting their own compensation, and the terms and
conditions of their own employment or contract. (Regulationl8702.4(aX3) and (b)(3);
Schectman Advice Letter, No. A-87-226.) These exceptions have formed the basis of our
advice about decisions regarding litigation in which public officials are named as
individual defenclants.a In certain circumstances, a difense and indemnification are part
of the "terms and conditions" of public office or employment. Therefore, because of
these regulatory exceptions, a public official may take part in decisions as to whether he
or she will be indemnified for general or special damages, whether counsel is to be
retained for his or her defense, and payment of attomeys' fees for his or her defense,
without violating the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. (Romney AdviceLetter,
No. A-99-292.) Mayor Pelayes' involvement in the decision whether to retain separate
counsel for his defense, at the City's expense, falls under this exception. However, this
exception does not apply to Councilmembers Hartz and Steffanus, who are plaintiffs
bringing suit in their private capacity.
3. What are these officials' economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from economic interests. You do not describe the specific economic interests of
Councilmembers Hartz and Steffanus and Mayor Pelayes. Nevertheless, in addition to
the specific economic interests separately listed in section 87103, a public official always
has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, and may have a conflict of
interest in any decision foreseeably resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal
expenses, income, assets or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family, in the
amormt of $250 or more over a l2-month period. (Regulation 18703.5.)
Mayor Pelayes
Government Code section 995 requires the City to defend and indemnifli Mayor
Pelayes in the pending litigation, upon his request, unless certain statutory exceptions
apply. (Stone v. Regents of the University of Caltfornia (1999) 77 Cal.App. 4th736;
Stewart v. City of Pismo Beach (1995) 35 Cal.App. 4th 1600; Rotnney Advice Letter,
supra.) You stated in a telephone conversation with Commission staff that Mayor
Pelayes requested the City to assume his defense. His costs of litigation, including
attomey's fees, is being borne by the City and he will be indemnified against any
decision awarding damages for his official conduct. Consequently, his economic interest
in his personal finances is not involved in decisions conceming this litigation. In the
absence of economic interests, the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act do not limit a
public official's involvement in a governmental decision. (Regulation 18700(bX2) -
(bX6); McHugh Advice Letter, No. A-01-219.)
a See CroninAdvice Letter, No. A-97-579; Schectman, supra; Smith Adviceletter, No. A-87-
305; Skolnik Advice Letter, No. I-9 I -240, superseded in part.)


File No. I-02-l4L
Page No. 5
Having concluded at step 2, above, that deciding whether to retain separate
counsel for Mayor Pelayes' defense does not involve his rnaking, participating in making,
or influenoing a governmental decision, and that for the reasons stated herein, that Mayor
Pelayes' personal finances will not be materially effected by any other litigation decisions
in the matter, it is clear without considering the remaining steps of our standard analysis
that he does not have a conflict of interest disqualifying him from participating in
govemmental decisions regarding this litigation.
Councilmembers Hartz and Steffanus
Courrcilmembers Hartz and Steffanus filed suit in their private capacity and not as
council tnembers or in a representative capacity for the City; indeed, the City is a co-
defendant. 'Ihus, they do not have recourse to the public treasury to defray their cost of
litigation or to indemnify them against damages. Their economic interest in their
personal finances would be implicated if these costs or expenses would increase or
decrease by $250 or more over a l2-month period as result of these litigation decisions.
4. WiIl these interests be directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decisions?
Regulation 18704.5 states that apublic official or his or her immediate family are
deemed to be directly involved in a govemmental decision which has any financial effect
on his or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate family. Thus, the
economic interest of Councilmembers Hartz and Steffanus in their personal finances will
be directly involved in these litigable decisions.
5. & 6. WiII there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
Councilmembers ff artz's and Steffanus' economic interests?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) An effect upon economic interests is considered reasonably foreseeable if
there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706.) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. Qn re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal finances
is material if it is at least $250 in any l}-month period. (Regulation 18705.5.) If it is
reasonably foreseeable that a litigable decision will have an effect on Councilmembers
Hartz's and Steffanus' fees or costs as plaintiffs, or damages awarded as defendants to a
countersuit, that will total $250 or more in a l2-month period, the reasonably foreseeable
effect of the decision on their economic interest in their personal finances is material and
they would have a conflict of interest potentially disquali$ring them from participating in
these decisions. (Aladjem Advice Letter, No. A-99-111.)


File No. I-02-141
Page No. 6
7. & 8. 'oPublic Generally" and 6(Legally Required Participation" Exceptions
Step seven is an exception that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. (Regulation 18707.) This exception does not apply in the
present instance, in which the council members' economic interest identified in our
analysis derives from their unique status as plaintiffs in litigation against the City and its
mayor.
Section 87101 permits an official who is otherwise disqualified from making a
governmental decision to participate in the decision when the official's participation is
legally required. This exception (step eight of our standard conflict-of-interest analysis)
is narrowly construed and applies only when it is impossible for the decision to be made
without the participation of a disqualified official. Consequently, the exception does not
apply when there exists an alternative source of decisionmaking consistent with the
statute authorizing the decision. (Regulation 18708.) Similarly, the exception does not
apply when the disqualified official's vote is merely needed to break a tie, or when a
quorum can be convened of other members of the ag.eficy who are not disqualified,
whether or not such other members are actually present.
Under this exception, not all of an agency's disqualified officials may participate.
Only the fewest number of disqualified persons necessary to constitute a quorum may
participate. (In re Hudson (1977) 4 FPPC Ops. 13.) We have advised that the best
method of selecting which disqualified official is to participate is by lot. (Aladjem
Advice Letter, supra.) All disqualified officials must participate in the selection process,
or the exception does not apply. (Heisinger Advice Letter,No. 4-95-333.)
Once it is determined which disqualified official will participate in a decision, that
official is selected for the duration of the proceeding and for all related matters, unless an
official previously disqualified no longer has a disqualifying financial interest in the
decision. Although an otherwise disqualified official may participate in a decision under
this exception, that participation does not extend to attempts to influence the outcome of
the matter "behind the scenes" by engaging in private discussions with other members of
the agency or the agency's staff. (Regulation 1870SOX4); Grunu,ald Advice Letter, No.
A-95-184.)
Once an otherwise disqualified official is selected to participate in a decision, that
official must: (1) disclose as a matter of official public record, the existence of his or her
disqualifying financial interest; (2) describe with particularity the nature of this financial
interest; and (3) state the reason an alternative source ofdecision does not exist.
(Regulation 18708(b); see generally Kunic v. Brea Redevelopment Agency (1997) 55
Cal.App.4th 5II, 522.)
In determining whether the exception applies in context of a vacancy, early advice
letters drelv a distinction between circumstances where it would be legally impossible to
fill the vacant seats, and where the seats were merely vacant until filled by the appointing


File No. I-02-l4L
Page No. 7
power.' (Hunter Advice Letter,No. 4-99-088.) However, in a 1999 opinion, the
Commission signaled a change from this prior advice and did not allow the distinction
alone to dictate the applicability of the exception. Instead, the Commission examined a
number of factors6 and concluded, in the case before it, that these factors prompted
application of the exception, even when the vacancy was the result of the political failure
of the appointing authority to fill the vacancy. (In re Tobtas (1999) 13 FPPC Op. 5.)
In the present case, the Adelanto City,Council has only fbur of its five seats filled.
You state in a telephone conversation with our staff that under the City's ordinances, the
vacant seat cannot be legally filled until a new member is elected at the City's next
general election; there is no alternate source of decision. Thus, with trvo disqualified
council members, the city council lacks a quorum to consider the pending litigation.
Based on the facts provided, the participation of either Councilmember Hartz or
Steffanus is legally required in order for the city council to reach a decision on matters
concerning this litigation. The city council may invoke the exception at regulation 18708
and randomly select either of these council members to participate in these decisions.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
L.
lick
Counsel,
Division
Enclosure
KIGje
I:\Adviceltrs\02-l4l
5 Phttlips Aclvice Letter, No. 4-82-111 ("legally required participation" did not apply because 3-
member vacancy on 7-member board was due to failure of political figures to appoint and confirm new
members.) Barbosa Advice Letter, No. 4-96-253 ("legally required participation" did not apply merely
because seats were vacant over a long period of time due to a failure to appoint.) Sunon Advice Letter,No.
A-90-643 (where one seat of the Irvine City Council could not be filled prior to an election, use of the
exception would be appropriate.) Schectman Advice Letter, No. A-92-198 (when two vacant seats on the
city council were due to be filled at the next election and there was no current ordinance in effect to appoint
temporary members, the "legally required participation" exception applied.)
6 These factors included: the nature of the decision; whethei there was an alternative method of
decisionmaking consistent with the purpose and functions of the particular agency; whether the agency
could have changed the quorum requirements or appointed alternative or interim members who could vote;
whether the decision had to be made within a specified time period; and the importance of the agency
moving forward.

reasonably foreseeable that a litigable decision will have an effect on Councilmembers
Hartz's and Steffanus' fees or costs as plaintiffs, or damages awarded as defendants to a
countersuit, that will total $250 or more in a l2-month period, the reasonably foreseeable
effect of the decision on their economic interest in their personal finances is material and
they would have a conflict of interest potentially disquali$ring them from participating in
these decisions. (Aladjem Advice Letter, No. A-99-111.)
7. & 8. 'oPublic Generally" and 6(Legally Required Participation" Exceptions
Step seven is an exception that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. (Regulation 18707.) This exception does not apply in the
present instance, in which the council members' economic interest identified in our
analysis derives from their unique status as plaintiffs in litigation against the City and its
mayor.
Section 87101 permits an official who is otherwise disqualified from making a
governmental decision to participate in the decision when the official's participation is
legally required. This exception (step eight of our standard conflict-of-interest analysis)
is narrowly construed and applies only when it is impossible for the decision to be made
without the participation of a disqualified official. Consequently, the exception does not
apply when there exists an alternative source of decisionmaking consistent with the
statute authorizing the decision. (Regulation 18708.) Similarly, the exception does not
apply when the disqualified official's vote is merely needed to break a tie, or when a
quorum can be convened of other members of the ag.eficy who are not disqualified,
whether or not such other members are actually present.
Under this exception, not all of an agency's disqualified officials may participate.
Only the fewest number of disqualified persons necessary to constitute a quorum may
participate. (In re Hudson (1977) 4 FPPC Ops. 13.) We have advised that the best
method of selecting which disqualified official is to participate is by lot. (Aladjem
Advice Letter, supra.) All disqualified officials must participate in the selection process,
or the exception does not apply. (Heisinger Advice Letter,No. 4-95-333.)
Once it is determined which disqualified official will participate in a decision, that
official is selected for the duration of the proceeding and for all related matters, unless an
official previously disqualified no longer has a disqualifying financial interest in the
decision. Although an otherwise disqualified official may participate in a decision under
this exception, that participation does not extend to attempts to influence the outcome of
the matter "behind the scenes" by engaging in private discussions with other members of
the agency or the agency's staff. (Regulation 1870SOX4); Grunu,ald Advice Letter, No.
A-95-184.)
Once an otherwise disqualified official is selected to participate in a decision, that
official must: (1) disclose as a matter of official public record, the existence of his or her
disqualifying financial interest; (2) describe with particularity the nature of this financial
interest; and (3) state the reason an alternative source ofdecision does not exist.
(Regulation 18708(b); see generally Kunic v. Brea Redevelopment Agency (1997) 55
Cal.App.4th 5II, 522.)
In determining whether the exception applies in context of a vacancy, early advice
letters drelv a distinction between circumstances where it would be legally impossible to
fill the vacant seats, and where the seats were merely vacant until filled by the appointing


File No. I-02-l4L
power.' (Hunter Advice Letter,No. 4-99-088.) However, in a 1999 opinion, the
Commission signaled a change from this prior advice and did not allow the distinction
alone to dictate the applicability of the exception. Instead, the Commission examined a
number of factors6 and concluded, in the case before it, that these factors prompted
application of the exception, even when the vacancy was the result of the political failure
of the appointing authority to fill the vacancy. (In re Tobtas (1999) 13 FPPC Op. 5.)
In the present case, the Adelanto City,Council has only fbur of its five seats filled.
You state in a telephone conversation with our staff that under the City's ordinances, the
vacant seat cannot be legally filled until a new member is elected at the City's next
general election; there is no alternate source of decision. Thus, with trvo disqualified
council members, the city council lacks a quorum to consider the pending litigation.
Based on the facts provided, the participation of either Councilmember Hartz or
Steffanus is legally required in order for the city council to reach a decision on matters
concerning this litigation. The city council may invoke the exception at regulation 18708
and randomly select either of these council members to participate in these decisions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do Mayor Tristan Pelayes and Councilmembers William Hartzand Gerald
Steffanus have a conflict of interest disqualifying them from participating in closed
session discussions and voting upon matters, including whether to retain separate counsel
for the mayor, regarding litigation filed by CouncilmembersHartz and Steffanus against
the City, and against fvfi. pefayes in his capacity as the City's mayot?2
' You also ask about the scope of the attomey-client privilege and the mechanics for the City's
waiver of the attorney client privilege . Since the Commission's jurisdiction is limited to matters arising
under the Act, we can offer no advice on your questions regarding this privilege.


File No. I-A2-l4l

CONCLUSION: Mayor Pelayes may participate in closed session discussions and vote upon
matters regarding this litigation, including the decision whether to retain separate counsel
to assume his defense. Govemment Code section 995 requires the City to defend Mayor
Pelayes in the pending litigation, upon his request, unless certain statutory exceptions
apply. Consequently, his economic interest in his personal finances is not involved in
litigation decisions brought before the city council. Moreover, the decision whether to
retain separate counsel is one that falls under the exception of regulation 18702.4 andis
considered a decision affecting his own compensation, or terms and conditions of his own
employment or contract. Under this exception, Mayor Pelayes is free to vote on the city
council's decision whether to obtain separate counsel for his defense in this litigation.
Councilmembers Hartz and Steffanus, as plaintiffs in the litigation, have a conflict
of interest ordinarily barring their involvement in governmental decisions regarding this
litigation if it is reasonably foreseeable flrat the decisions will result in a material
financial effect on their personal finances. Ho\rrever, under the facts provided the City
will lack a quorum if both of these council members are ba:red from involvement. Under
the "legally required participation" exception, one of these two council members may be
randomly selected to be involved in the governmental decisions regarding this litigation.3
